A **topological insulator** is a material whose interior behaves as an [electrical insulator](/wiki/Electrical_insulator "Electrical insulator") while its surface behaves as an [electrical conductor](/wiki/Electrical_conductivity "Electrical conductivity"),[[3]](#cite_note-KM-3) meaning that electrons can only move along the surface of the material.

A topological insulator is an insulator for the same reason a "[trivial](/wiki/Triviality_(mathematics) "Triviality (mathematics)")" (ordinary) insulator is: there exists an [energy gap](/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap") between the [valence and conduction bands](/wiki/Valence_and_conduction_bands "Valence and conduction bands") of the material. But in a topological insulator, these bands are, in an informal sense, "twisted", relative to a trivial insulator.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The topological insulator cannot be [continuously](/wiki/Continuous_function "Continuous function") transformed into a trivial one without untwisting the bands, which closes the band gap and creates a conducting state. Thus, due to the continuity of the underlying field, the [border](/wiki/Surface_state "Surface state") of a topological insulator with a trivial insulator (including [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum"), which is topologically trivial) is forced to support conducting [edge states](/wiki/Edge_states "Edge states").[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5)

Since this results from a global property of the topological insulator's [band structure](/wiki/Band_structure "Band structure"), local (symmetry-preserving) perturbations cannot damage this surface state.[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6) This is unique to topological insulators: while ordinary insulators can also support conductive surface states, only the surface states of topological insulators have this robustness property.

This leads to a more formal definition of a topological insulator: an insulator which cannot be [adiabatically](/wiki/Adiabatic_theorem "Adiabatic theorem") transformed into an ordinary insulator without passing through an intermediate conducting state.[[5]](#cite_note-:10-5) In other words, topological insulators and trivial insulators are separate regions in the [phase diagram](/wiki/Phase_diagram "Phase diagram"), connected only by conducting phases. In this way, topological insulators provide an example of a [state of matter](/wiki/State_of_matter "State of matter") not described by the [Landau symmetry-breaking theory](/wiki/Landau_theory "Landau theory") that defines ordinary states of matter.[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6)

The properties of topological insulators and their surface states are highly dependent on both the dimension of the material and its underlying [symmetries](/wiki/Symmetry_in_quantum_mechanics "Symmetry in quantum mechanics"), and can be classified using the so-called [periodic table of topological insulators](/wiki/Periodic_table_of_topological_invariants "Periodic table of topological invariants"). Some combinations of dimension and symmetries forbid topological insulators completely.[[7]](#cite_note-:12-7) All topological insulators have at least [U(1) symmetry](/wiki/U(1) "U(1)") from [particle number](/wiki/Particle_number "Particle number") conservation, and often have [time-reversal symmetry](/wiki/Time-reversal_symmetry "Time-reversal symmetry") from the absence of a magnetic field. In this way, topological insulators are an example of [symmetry-protected topological order](/wiki/Symmetry-protected_topological_order "Symmetry-protected topological order").[[8]](#cite_note-8) So-called "topological invariants", taking values in

The surface states of topological insulators can have exotic properties. For example, in time-reversal symmetric 3D topological insulators, surface states have their [spin](/wiki/Spin_quantum_number "Spin quantum number") locked at a right-angle to their momentum (spin-momentum locking). At a given energy the only other available electronic states have different spin, so ["U"-turn scattering](/wiki/Umklapp_scattering "Umklapp scattering") is strongly suppressed and conduction on the surface is highly metallic.

Despite their origin in quantum mechanical systems, analogues of topological insulators can also be found in classical media. There exist [photonic](/wiki/Photonic_topological_insulator "Photonic topological insulator"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) [magnetic](/wiki/Magnetic_topological_insulators "Magnetic topological insulators"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) and [acoustic](/wiki/Acoustics "Acoustics")[[11]](#cite_note-11) topological insulators, among others.

## Prediction

The first models of 3D topological insulators were proposed by B. A. Volkov and O. A. Pankratov in 1985,[[12]](#cite_note-Volkov85-12)
and subsequently by Pankratov, S. V. Pakhomov, and Volkov in 1987.[[13]](#cite_note-Pankratov87-13) Gapless 2D Dirac states were shown to exist at the band inversion contact in [PbTe](/wiki/PbTe "PbTe")/[SnTe](/wiki/SnTe "SnTe")[[12]](#cite_note-Volkov85-12) and [HgTe](/wiki/HgTe "HgTe")/[CdTe](/wiki/CdTe "CdTe")[[13]](#cite_note-Pankratov87-13) heterostructures.
Existence of interface Dirac states in HgTe/CdTe was experimentally verified by [Laurens W. Molenkamp's](/wiki/Laurens_W._Molenkamp "Laurens W. Molenkamp") group in 2D topological insulators in 2007.[[14]](#cite_note-König07-14)

Later sets of theoretical models for the 2D topological insulator (also known as the quantum spin Hall insulators) were proposed by [Charles L. Kane](/wiki/Charles_L._Kane "Charles L. Kane") and [Eugene J. Mele](/wiki/Eugene_J._Mele "Eugene J. Mele") in 2005,[[15]](#cite_note-15) and also by [B. Andrei Bernevig](/wiki/B._Andrei_Bernevig "B. Andrei Bernevig") and [Shoucheng Zhang](/wiki/Shoucheng_Zhang "Shoucheng Zhang") in 2006.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The

In 2007, it was predicted that 3D topological insulators might be found in binary compounds involving [bismuth](/wiki/Bismuth "Bismuth"),[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) and in particular "strong topological insulators" exist that cannot be reduced to multiple copies of the [quantum spin Hall state](/wiki/Quantum_spin_Hall_effect "Quantum spin Hall effect").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Properties and applications

Spin-momentum locking[[24]](#cite_note-Hsieh09-24) in the topological insulator allows symmetry-protected surface states to host [Majorana particles](/wiki/Majorana_particle "Majorana particle") if superconductivity is induced on the surface of 3D topological insulators via proximity effects.[[25]](#cite_note-25) (Note that Majorana zero-mode can also appear without topological insulators.[[26]](#cite_note-Potter-26)) The non-trivialness of topological insulators is encoded in the existence of a gas of [helical Dirac fermions](/wiki/Helical_Dirac_fermion "Helical Dirac fermion"). Dirac particles which behave like massless relativistic fermions have been observed in 3D topological insulators. Note that the gapless surface states of topological insulators differ from those in the [quantum Hall effect](/wiki/Quantum_Hall_effect "Quantum Hall effect"): the gapless surface states of topological insulators are symmetry-protected (i.e., not topological), while the gapless surface states in quantum Hall effect are topological (i.e., robust against any local perturbations that can break all the symmetries). The most promising applications of topological insulators are spintronic devices and dissipationless [transistors](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor") for [quantum computers](/wiki/Quantum_computer "Quantum computer") based on the [quantum Hall effect](/wiki/Quantum_spin_Hall_effect "Quantum spin Hall effect")[[14]](#cite_note-König07-14) and [quantum anomalous Hall effect](/wiki/Quantum_anomalous_Hall_effect "Quantum anomalous Hall effect").[[31]](#cite_note-31) In addition, topological insulator materials have also found practical applications in advanced [magnetoelectronic](/wiki/Magnetoelectronics "Magnetoelectronics") and [optoelectronic](/wiki/Optoelectronic "Optoelectronic") devices.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Thermoelectrics

Some of the most well-known topological insulators are also [thermoelectric materials](/wiki/Thermoelectric_materials "Thermoelectric materials"), such as Bi2Te3 and its alloys with Bi2Se3 (n-type thermoelectrics) and Sb2Te3 (p-type thermoelectrics).[[34]](#cite_note-34) High thermoelectric power conversion efficiency is realized in materials with low thermal conductivity, high electrical conductivity, and high [Seebeck coefficient](/wiki/Seebeck_coefficient "Seebeck coefficient") (i.e., the incremental change in voltage due to an incremental change in temperature). Topological insulators are often composed of heavy atoms, which tends to lower thermal conductivity and are therefore beneficial for thermoelectrics. A recent study also showed that good electrical characteristics (i.e., high electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient) can arise in topological insulators due to warping of the bulk band structure, which is driven by band inversion.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Often, the electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient are conflicting properties of thermoelectrics and difficult to optimize simultaneously. Band warping, induced by band inversion in a topological insulator, can mediate the two properties by reducing the effective mass of electrons/holes and increasing the valley degeneracy (i.e., the number of electronic bands that are contributing to charge transport). As a result, topological insulators are generally interesting candidates for thermoelectric applications.

### Two-band Hamiltonians

The topological insulators that are easiest to describe are periodic crystalline insulators with two degrees of freedom (e.g. two orbitals) per [unit cell](/wiki/Unit_cell "Unit cell"). Although a lot of the following treatment is only true for those systems, it is easily generalizable to any crystalline topological insulator. Some notable examples of two-band systems are [graphene](/wiki/Graphene "Graphene") and the [Su-Shrieffer-Heeger model](/wiki/Su%E2%80%93Schrieffer%E2%80%93Heeger_model "Su–Schrieffer–Heeger model") (describing [polyacetylene](/wiki/Polyacetylene "Polyacetylene")). # A Beginner’s Guide to Topological Materials

## The stuff of 2016’s Nobel Prize in Physics could become the logic in future computers and consumer electronics

Charles Q. Choi is a contributing editor for IEEE Spectrum.

![Image of tantalum arsenide.](https://spectrum.ieee.org/media-library/image-of-tantalum-arsenide.jpg?id=27044630&width=1200&height=939)

**Ever since a** new class of ­materials called [topological insulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/1310.0255) was first created—a discovery that helped win the [Nobel Prize in Physics in 2016](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/physics/2016/press-release/)—researchers have been intrigued by the possibilities for electronics applications such as ultralow-energy transistors, [cancer-scanning lasers](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep01998)­, and [free-space communication beyond 5G](https://www.osapublishing.org/oe/fulltext.cfm?uri=oe-20-9-9396&id=231832). [Topological](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/topological) insulators’ unusual name stems from their being insulating on the inside and conductive on the outside: On the outer boundaries of a topological insulator, electricity or (in some cases) light flows readily around corners and defects, and with virtually no losses.

Amazingly, [topological insulators](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/topological-insulators) appear to be only the first of a possible generation of exotic electrical and optical semimetals, [superconductors](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/superconductors), and other forms of matter. As confusing as these strange and sometimes outlandish compounds might be at the moment, researchers have found that these materials can possess exceptional properties that could be developed into future technologies.

[Topology](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/topology) is the branch of mathematics that explores the nature of shapes independent of deformation. For instance, an object shaped like a doughnut can be deformed into the shape of a mug, so that the doughnut’s hole becomes the hole in the cup’s handle. However, the object couldn’t lose the hole without changing into a fundamentally different shape.

Employing insights from topology, researchers developed the first electronic topological insulators in 2007. [Electrons](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/electrons) zipping along the edges or surfaces of these materials are “topologically protected,” meaning that the patterns in which the electrons flow will stay unchanged in the face of any disturbances they might encounter.

How might [topological materials](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/topological-materials) find their way into the electronics and [photonics](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/photonics) of tomorrow? Below are a few possible routes.

**Electronic topological insulators**

Scientists in [Australia](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/australia) suggest that ­transistors based on electronic topological insulators could help computers save substantial amounts of energy. “We expect that topological transistors could replace conventional semiconductor transistors and use much less energy for the same size transistor,” says physicist Michael Fuhrer at Monash University, in Melbourne.

To represent data as ones and zeroes, electronics switch transistors between one electric state and the other, and this switching requires a minimum amount of voltage. The researchers explored replacing the conventional [semiconductors](https://spectrum.ieee.org/topic/semiconductors/) used to make transistors with electronic topological insulators. When these materials behave as they normally do, their conductive edges can function as a transistor’s “on” state. But when an electric field is applied, they stop acting like electronic topological insulators and so no longer possess conductive edges, thus serving as a transistor’s “off” state.

By using the right topological materials, the scientists calculated that topological transistors could consume just half the voltage and [one-quarter the energy of standard transistors](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.nanolett.1c00378). “Computing today uses 8 to 10 percent of the world’s electricity, and is doubling every decade,” Fuhrer says. “So we need a new technology to make computer chips more efficient. Topological transistors could do this.”

One possible candidate for topological transistors is [bismuthene](https://www.azom.com/article.aspx?ArticleID=15699), a single layer of [bismuth](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/bismuth) atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice. Researchers have only just begun studying bismuthene in the laboratory, so it hasn’t made it into transistors yet. Other materials have not yet been synthesized. “This work takes a lot of time, and we don’t expect that the transistors we envision will be in your [iPhone](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/iphone) next year, or perhaps even in this decade,” Fuhrer says.

**Photonic topological insulators**

[In 2009](https://www.nature.com/articles/nature08293), [scientists developed](/semiconductors/optoelectronics/topological-photonics-what-it-is-why-we-need-it) photonic topological insulators, in which light is similarly topologically protected. The structures of these materials lead specific wavelengths of light to flow along their outsides without losses or scattering, even around corners and imperfections.

The first topological insulators to find practical use may in fact be photonic, not electronic. One possible application could be lasers that incorporate topological protection, which may display significantly better efficiency and robustness against defects than conventional devices. Such benefits will boost the number and consistency of usable devices after fabrication, says electrical and optical engineer [Qi Jie Wang](https://personal.ntu.edu.sg/qjwang/home.html) at [Nanyang Technological University](https://www.ntu.edu.sg), in [Singapore](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/singapore).

The scientists began with a wafer made of layers of [gallium arsenide](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/gallium-arsenide) and [aluminum gallium arsenide](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32051601/). When electrically charged, the wafer emitted bright light. The researchers drilled an array of holes into the wafer, each resembling an equilateral triangle with its corners trimmed off. Around this lattice the researchers drilled additional holes of the same shape but oriented in the opposite direction. The light from the wafer flowed along the boundary between the different sets of holes and emerged from nearby channels as laser beams. This flow proved robust against defects, including the extra holes the scientists drilled. “We are able to create photonic structures that light can pass through without scattering losses or reflections,” Wang says.

![Image of a single layer of bismuth atoms.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20600%20776'%3E%3C/svg%3E)Bismuthene, a single layer of bismuth atoms in a honeycombed sheet, could be the stuff of future topological transistors.Emily Cooper

![Image of a single layer of bismuth atoms.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20600%20776'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

This laser works in [terahertz](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/terahertz) frequencies, which are useful for [cancer](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/cancer) screening and airport security scans. Wang and his colleagues are currently exploring topological lasers using layers of indium gallium arsenide and indium aluminum arsenide sandwiched together to emit mid-infrared wavelengths, useful for applications such as detecting and analyzing air pollutants, lidar sensors, or free-space communication beyond [5G](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/5g). He notes that the COVID-19 pandemic has delayed experimental work, however.

**Topological superconductors**

Whereas topological insulators possess topologically protected electrons or photons on their exteriors, an unusual class of superconductors known as topological superconductors may boast elusive theoretical particles on their surfaces that could drive a major advance in [quantum computing](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/quantum-computing).

[Topological superconductors](https://arxiv.org/abs/1608.03395) are often made of a [superconducting](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/superconducting) metal coupled with a semiconductor. Interactions between these materials can generate [Majorana fermions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Majorana_fermion), long-theorized particles that are their own antiparticles.

[Majorana fermions](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/majorana-fermions) could find use as quantum bits, or qubits, which lie at the heart of most quantum ­computers—machines that can theoretically perform more computations in an instant than there are atoms in the universe. Qubits are typically fragile, but the Majorana fermions of topological superconductors could prove topologically protected against disturbances, a feature that scientists have suggested could lead to a practical quantum computer. “Topological qubits are the most important reason people are interested in topological superconductivity,” says ­condensed-matter theorist Ruixing Zhang at the University of ­Maryland, College Park.

However, so far no conclusive proof has emerged for the existence of either topological superconductors or ­Majorana fermions, Zhang says. A 2018 Microsoft-backed study claimed to find strong evidence of both, but this work [was ultimately retracted in 2021](/tech-talk/computing/hardware/majorana-microsoft-backed-quantum-computer-research-retracted).

Still, researchers hold out hopes of confirming the existence of topological superconductors. Zhang and his colleagues suggest examining [thin films of iron-based superconductors](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.126.137001), while others suggest [materials such as graphene](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202008113). “I’m very optimistic we’ll realize Majorana physics in the coming years,” Zhang says.

**Topological semimetals**

Topological semimetals sit between metals and insulators in terms of such properties as their ability to conduct electricity or heat. Increasingly, scientists around the world find that these materials can possess extraordinary features, such as virtually dissipationless electric currents and the ability to convert more light to electricity than any other material can, which hint at a wide range of potential applications, such as ultralow-power electronics and generating electricity from [waste heat](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/waste-heat).

A dizzying variety of topological semimetals exist, such as [Dirac semimetals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_matter), [Weyl semimetals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weyl_semimetal), [multifold fermion semimetals](https://www.pnas.org/content/117/44/27104), and more, each of which are topologically different from the others. Whereas conventional semimetals can easily convert to either metals or insulators with a change in temperature or a slight tweak of their chemical makeup, topological semimetals stubbornly retain their semimetallic nature despite changes to temperature or composition.

Just like [graphene](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/graphene), electric currents can flow in topological semimetals with virtually zero dissipation of energy, potentially making them useful for ultralow-power electronics, says physicist [Masaki Uchida](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=m0Nb6hUAAAAJ&hl=en) at the [Tokyo Institute of Technology](https://www.titech.ac.jp/english). At the same time, researchers can theoretically vary the thicknesses of topological semimetals to tune their properties, whereas atomically thin graphene has finite thickness and thus less flexibility for design ­purposes, says physicist [Yee Sin Ang](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=eOklqWYAAAAJ&hl=en) at the [Singapore University of Technology and Design](https://www.sutd.edu.sg).

Topological semimetals can also display unexpected properties—for example, physicist [Ken Burch](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=86AmwPgAAAAJ&hl=en) at [Boston College](https://www.bc.edu) and his colleagues found that [tantalum](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/tantalum) arsenide can intrinsically generate more than 10 times as much electric current from light as [any other material](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41563-019-0297-4). This effect occurs with mid-­infrared light, which suggests that tantalum arsenide could find use in chemical and thermal imaging. “You could also imagine taking infrared [radiation](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/radiation) emitted as waste energy off hot objects and converting it to useful electricity,” Burch says.

Scientists may have overlooked the dramatic features of many topological semimetals for decades. Theoretical physicist [Benjamin Wieder](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=BDwvO84AAAAJ&hl=en) at MIT and his colleagues just recently discovered such properties in [cobalt](https://spectrum.ieee.org/tag/cobalt) monosilicide and [similar materials](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.119.206402), which researchers have studied for nearly 70 years.

“The future of topological materials discovery and applications might not be in the design of new materials, but in...the rediscovery of interesting materials with overlooked properties,” Wieder says. “The next highly desirable solid-state material might be hiding in a 70-year-old paper, if you know where to look.”

[Charles Q. Choi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/charlesqchoi) is a science reporter who contributes regularly to *IEEE Spectrum*. He has written for *Scientific American*, *The New York Times*, *Wired*, and *Science*, among others.

## What Are Topological Insulators?

**Topological insulators** represent an intriguing state of matter, bridging the gap between insulators and conductors in a way that defies conventional physics. These materials have the novel ability to conduct electricity on their surface or edges while maintaining insulation within their bulk. The exploration of topological insulators has not only expanded our understanding of quantum physics but also opened pathways for advancements in electronics and quantum computing.

### Understanding the Fundamentals of Topological Insulators

At the heart of understanding **topological insulators** is the concept of **band structure**, which in simple terms, describes the energy levels that electrons can occupy in a solid. Unlike ordinary insulators and conductors, topological insulators exhibit a unique band structure that allows for conducting states on the material's surface, even though their interior acts as an insulator. This behaviour is governed by the topology of the insulator's electron wave functions, a property that remains unchanged even when the material is deformed, as long as the deformation doesn't close the band gap.

**Band structure:** A representation of the allowed energy levels within a solid that electrons can occupy. It's crucial for determining a material's electrical properties.

**Example:** Imagine wrapping a rubber band around your fingers. Regardless of how you stretch or twist the rubber band (without breaking it), its fundamental looped nature doesn't change. Similarly, the surface states of topological insulators maintain their conducting properties under various deformations, showcasing their robustness.

The study of topological insulators also highlights the significance of **spin-orbit coupling**, which is a quantum mechanical phenomenon where an electron's spin (its intrinsic form of angular momentum) is linked with its orbital motion around an atom. This coupling plays a pivotal role in the existence of conducting surface states, making these materials not just electrically conductive on the surface but also sensitive to the direction of an electron's spin. This characteristic opens the door to potential applications in spintronics, a field that aims to exploit electron spin in electronic devices.

**Spin-orbit coupling:** A quantum mechanical phenomenon where an electron's spin is coupled with its orbital motion around an atom, affecting the energy levels and magnetic properties of the material.

### Key Properties and Characteristics of Topological Insulators

**Topological insulators** are defined by their unique set of properties and characteristics that differentiate them from other materials. Understanding these key aspects is crucial for grasping the potential applications and importance of these materials in modern science and technology. Here are the main properties and characteristics of topological insulators:

A deeper look into the **[quantum Hall effect](/en-us/explanations/math/theoretical-and-mathematical-physics/quantum-hall-effect/)**, a phenomenon observed in two-dimensional electron systems under strong magnetic fields, reveals the roots of topological insulators. This effect was the first physical realization that topological states could exist, showcasing quantised Hall conductance as a topological invariant. It is fascinating to see how concepts from the [quantum Hall effect](/en-us/explanations/math/theoretical-and-mathematical-physics/quantum-hall-effect/) have evolved and found new realisations in topological insulators, expanding our understanding of quantum physics and materials science.

## 2D Topological Insulator Explained

Exploring the concept of 2D Topological Insulators offers intriguing insights into the cutting-edge of materials science. These two-dimensional materials exhibit extraordinary physical properties that have far-reaching implications for technology and electronics. The heart of their uniqueness lies in their ability to conduct electricity on their edges while being insulating in their interior, a phenomenon rooted deeply in quantum physics.Understanding 2D topological insulators necessitates a grasp of advanced physics concepts, yet the basic principles are accessible and make for a captivating study.

### The Basics of 2D Topological Insulator

To dive into the basics of 2D topological insulators, it's crucial to discuss the concept of **quantum spin Hall effect**. This quantum phenomenon underlies the operational principle of 2D topological insulators. Unlike ordinary materials, where electric current comprises electrons moving in a random scattering of directions, the quantum spin Hall effect ensures that electrons in 2D topological insulators move along the edges of the material in a unidirectional flow, with their spin aligned to their direction of motion.This unique transport property is due to the existence of **edge states** at the boundary between different topological phases of a material, serving as conduits for electron flow free of backscattering from impurities. This leads to a perfect conductance channel, even in the presence of disorder.

**Quantum spin Hall effect:** A state of matter that enables electrons to move along the edges of a material without energy loss due to scattering, facilitated by their spin. It is one of the hallmark qualities of a 2D topological insulator.

**Example:** Imagine a tightly packed crowd in a stadium moving chaotically to exit. Now imagine a dedicated pathway around the edges where individuals can move swiftly without any obstruction. The latter scenario mirrors the edge states in 2D topological insulators, enabling electrons to move efficiently along the material's edges.

The robustness against backscattering in 2D topological insulators makes them incredibly efficient for electrical conductivity, mirroring a 'perfect' wire with minimal energy loss.

The mechanism behind these topological edge states can be described by the concept of **band inversion**, a process where the energy levels of the conducting and valence bands in a material are swapped due to strong spin-orbit coupling. This band inversion is a pivotal event leading to the creation of protected edge states that are impervious to external perturbations such as impurities or defects.The understanding of these processes is founded on **Kane-Mele model**, a theoretical framework that first predicted the quantum spin Hall effect in graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice.

### Practical Applications and Examples of 2D Topological Insulators

The peculiar properties of 2D topological insulators unlock a plethora of practical applications, especially in the realm of electronics and quantum computing. Their robust and efficient edge conduction pathways could revolutionize how electronic devices are designed and constructed, leading to gadgets that are more energy-efficient, faster, and capable of processing information in fundamentally new ways.One of the most promising applications of 2D topological insulators is in the development of **spintronics**. This technology leverages the electron spin, in addition to its charge, for data processing. Since 2D topological insulators inherently control electron spin through the alignment along their edges, they offer an ideal platform for spintronic devices.

**Example:** A futuristic application of 2D topological insulators is in the creation of quantum computers. Unlike traditional computers, which use bits as the smallest unit of information, quantum computers use qubits. The edge states of 2D topological insulators, with their controlled electron movement and spin, could potentially serve as qubits, making quantum computing more feasible.

Beyond spintronics and quantum computing, 2D topological insulators hold potential for advancing energy technologies, notably in the development of more efficient solar cells. The unique electrical properties of these materials can be harnessed to create topological solar cells with significantly higher efficiency by minimising energy loss that typically occurs in conventional solar cells. This application, although still in its infancy, exemplifies the broad impact that understanding and utilising topological insulators could have on energy solutions worldwide.

The practicality of implementing 2D topological insulators in technology hinges on overcoming current manufacturing challenges, especially in fabricating large-scale, defect-free materials that preserve the unique edge states.

## 3D Topological Insulator Insight

3D topological insulators have become a major focus within the field of condensed matter physics, known for their distinctive ability to conduct electricity on their surfaces while remaining insulating in their bulk. This fascinating dichotomy is not just a theoretical curiosity but holds promising applications in various technological advancements, especially in electronics and quantum computing.The understanding of 3D topological insulators requires a deep dive into quantum mechanics and the sophisticated behaviour of electrons in solid materials.

### Introduction to 3D Topological Insulator

3D topological insulators are a new state of matter, exhibiting unique electric properties that distinguish them from conventional insulators and conductors. Their surfaces are capable of conducting electricity without dissipation, while their interior volumes do not conduct electricity. This peculiar characteristic arises from the material's topological properties, which are immune to minor changes or impurities.The advent of 3D topological insulators revolves around the concept of **time-reversal symmetry** and strong spin-orbit interaction, leading to protected surface states that are robust against environmental disruptions.

**Time-Reversal Symmetry:** A fundamental principle in physics where the basic equations governing a system remain invariant when the direction of time is reversed. In the context of 3D topological insulators, this means that the system's properties are preserved even if the direction of all motions were reversed.

**Example:** Consider a ball thrown into the air. Its motion, subjected to gravity, is time-reversible - meaning if you could record and play its trajectory backwards, it would obey the same laws of motion. Similarly, in a 3D topological insulator, electrons moving on the surface states obey time-reversal symmetry, maintaining their unique conducting properties under reversed conditions.

It is the combination of time-reversal symmetry and strong spin-orbit coupling that gives 3D topological insulators their robust, protected surface states, capable of conducting electricity with high efficiency.

Mathematically, the behaviour of electrons in 3D topological insulators is described using **Z2 topological invariants**. These invariants provide a way to categorise materials based on their electronic band structures and are crucial for predicting whether a material will exhibit topological insulating properties. The formula for calculating Z2 is derived from the integral of the Berry curvature across the Brillouin zone, a complex mathematical formulation beyond the scope of this introduction but fundamental to the field of topological insulators.

### The Role of 3D Topological Insulators in Advanced Physics

The significance of 3D topological insulators stretches far beyond [theoretical physics](/en-us/explanations/math/theoretical-and-mathematical-physics/theoretical-physics/), paving the way for revolutionary applications in technology. Their unique electronic properties have the potential to transform the field of electronics, particularly in the development of low-power, high-speed computing devices and spintronics, where the spin of electrons, rather than their charge, is manipulated for data storage and transfer.In addition, researchers are investigating the utility of 3D topological insulators in creating more efficient and stable quantum computers. The protected surface states of these materials provide an ideal platform for quantum bits (qubits) that are less susceptible to environmental noise, a major hurdle in current quantum computing architectures.

Furthermore, 3D topological insulators are at the forefront of exploring new phenomena in quantum physics, such as the discovery of Majorana fermions - particles that are their own antiparticles. These exotic particles have potential applications in fault-tolerant quantum computing due to their non-Abelian statistics, making them particularly suitable for [quantum error correction](/en-us/explanations/math/theoretical-and-mathematical-physics/quantum-error-correction/).The exploration of topological phases and their transition points also offers insight into the fundamental workings of the quantum world, guiding the way to novel materials with tailored electronic properties for specific applications.

One intriguing aspect of research in 3D topological insulators is the study of Weyl semimetals. These materials share similarities with topological insulators, as they possess conductive surface states, but also feature peculiar 3D Dirac points called Weyl points. These points, where valence and conduction bands meet, give rise to exotic physical phenomena, such as extremely high mobility of electrons and anomalous Hall effects, indicating the potential for applications in ultra-fast electronic devices and sensors.The interplay between 3D topological insulators and Weyl semimetals underscores the richness of quantum materials science, promising a future where the manipulation of topological properties leads to the next generation of technological innovations.

## Diverse Types of Topological Insulators

Topological insulators are a group of materials with unique electronic properties that have captivated scientists and engineers alike. They are distinguished by their ability to conduct electricity on their surfaces while remaining insulating in their bulk. This fascinating property stems from the specific topological order of these materials, making them a hotbed for groundbreaking research and potential technological applications.In this exploration, you'll learn about several diverse types of topological insulators, each with its own set of properties and applications.

### Magnetic Topological Insulator: A Closer Look

Magnetic topological insulators are a variant of topological insulators that incorporate magnetic elements within their structure. This inclusion of magnetic elements endows these materials with intriguing magnetic properties in addition to their topological insulating nature. Unlike conventional topological insulators, magnetic topological insulators can break time-reversal symmetry while still maintaining robust surface states that are protected by other symmetries.The interaction between magnetism and topology in these materials opens up new avenues for applications in quantum computing and spintronics, where the manipulation of electron spin, rather than charge, could lead to more efficient devices.

**Magnetic Topological Insulator:** A class of topological insulators that incorporate magnetic elements, breaking time-reversal symmetry but preserving surface states through other symmetries.

**Example:** An example of a magnetic topological insulator is bismuth-antimony (Bi-Sb) alloy doped with manganese (Mn). This combination allows the material to exhibit both magnetic properties and topologically protected surface states.

### Exploring Higher-Order Topological Insulators

Higher-order topological insulators (HOTIs) represent a further advancement in the classification of topological insulators. While conventional topological insulators have conducting states on their one-dimensional (1D) edges or two-dimensional (2D) surfaces, HOTIs extend this concept to include insulating states that are protected by topology on their corners or hinges, representing zero-dimensional (0D) or one-dimensional (1D) modes, respectively.The theoretical foundation and experimental observation of HOTIs have led to a deeper understanding of the role of symmetry in topological phases of matter, offering prospects for novel quantum devices and platforms for topological quantum computing.

**Higher-Order Topological Insulators (HOTIs):** A new phase of topological insulators that possess protected conducting states not only on their outermost boundaries but also at corners or hinges, depending on their dimensionality.

### Chern Number Topological Insulator: What You Need to Know

Chern number topological insulators are characterised by their nontrivial topological invariants, known as Chern numbers, which mathematically describe the winding of electron wavefunctions in these materials. The Chern number is integral to understanding the quantum Hall effect and its extension, the quantum anomalous Hall effect, observed in certain topological insulators without the need for an external magnetic field.The presence of a nonzero Chern number signifies that a material has a quantized conductance, making these insulators highly sought after for potential applications in low-power electronic devices and quantum computing.

**Chern Number Topological Insulator:** A type of topological insulator that exhibits a nontrivial topological invariant, known as the Chern number, indicative of a quantised conductance even without an external magnetic field.

**Example:** A well-known example of a Chern number topological insulator is the quantum anomalous Hall insulator, which exhibits quantised Hall conductance without applying an external magnetic field. This is achieved through intrinsic magnetic ordering within the material.

Exploring the mathematical underpinnings, the Chern number \(C\_1\) for a two-dimensional material is defined by the formula \[C\_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int\_{BZ} d\mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{F(\mathbf{k})}\], where \(BZ\) denotes the Brillouin zone, \(\mathbf{k}\) is the wave vector, and \(\mathbf{F(\mathbf{k})}\) represents the Berry curvature. This integral quantifies the topological order and determines the existence of edge states that are responsible for the unique conducting properties of these materials. Understanding the role of Chern numbers in classifying topological phases extends far beyond topological insulators to encompass a wide array of materials with topological properties.

## Abstract

Within the broad and deep field of topological materials, there are an ever-increasing number of materials that harbor topological phases. While condensed matter physics continues to probe the exotic physical properties resulting from the existence of topological phases in new materials, there exists a suite of “well-known” topological materials in which the physical properties are well-characterized, such as Bi2Se3 and Bi2Te3. In this context, it is then appropriate to ask if the unique properties of well-explored topological materials may have a role to play in applications that form the basis of a new paradigm in information processing devices and architectures. To accomplish such a transition from physical novelty to application based material, the potential of topological materials must be disseminated beyond the reach of condensed matter to engender interest in diverse areas such as: electrical engineering, materials science, and applied physics. Accordingly, in this review, we assess the state of current electronic device applications and contemplate the future prospects of topological materials from an applied perspective. More specifically, we will review the application of topological materials to the general areas of electronic and magnetic device technologies with the goal of elucidating the potential utility of well-characterized topological materials in future information processing applications.

## Introduction

To date, the majority of information processing is done using complementary metal oxide semiconductors (CMOSs), an architecture based on a series of interconnected semiconductor devices each performing a different function that aids in the overall goal of processing digital information. The societal demand for smaller-sized electronic devices such as computers and cellular phones with improved functionality has forced not only the sizes of the constituent components of CMOS information processing to rapidly and inexorably shrink but also for the operational frequencies to increase. The required increases in device performance have led traditional semiconductor device technology to reach fundamental, and perhaps ultimately insurmountable, limits of several key quantities, namely, size, weight, and power (SWaP). Specifically in terms of information processing architectures, the most crucial of the SWaP limitations is that of power consumption. In order to continue the march toward ever smaller yet more efficient information processing systems, the architectures have undergone major renovations such as moving to multiple logical core processing in order to circumvent the limitations in power consumption. Yet, the imposition of multicore processing has resulted in a slower rate of improvement than previously demonstrated by increases allotted by transistor scaling[1](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR1 "Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), The International Roadmap for Devices and Systems (IRDS). 2018 (2018)."). Therefore, finding a methodology to reduce the SWaP of constituent CMOS devices while increasing overall operational efficiency represents an eminent international grand challenge.

While the need to abandon the traditional architectures associated with transistor scaling is generally accepted, the material in which to implement these new architectures is very much an open debate. The requirements for low-power and high-frequency responses have not been satisfied despite many years of intense development. Such realization thereby necessitates the introduction of new classes of materials with correspondingly new physical responses compared to those of traditional semiconducting compounds. One may search for new materials and associated excitations by re-examining previously well-understood materials. In doing so, we recall that the discovery and subsequent classification of different phases of matter in terms of the symmetries present, based on the theory by Landau and Lifshitz[2](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR2 "Lifshitz, E. M. & Pitaevskii, L. P. Statistical Physics (Pergamon Press, 1980)."),[3](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR3 "Nussinov, Z. & Ortiz, G. A symmetry principle for topological order. Ann. Phys. 324, 977 (2009)."), has been at the forefront of condensed matter physics for >70 years. Nevertheless, the theory of phase transitions in condensed matter systems required an extension to include the ideas of topological phase transitions and topological order to account for experimental observations of the integer[4](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR4 "Klitzing, K. V., Dorda, G. & Pepper, M. New method for high-accuracy determination of the fine-structure constant based on quantized Hall conductance. Phys. Rev. Lett. 45, 494 (1980)."),[5](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR5 "Thouless, D. J., Kohmoto, M., Nightingale, M. P. & den Nijs, M. Quantized Hall conductance in a two-dimensional periodic potential. Phys. Rev. Lett. 49, 405 (1982).") and fractional quantum Hall regime[6](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR6 "Stormer, H. L., Tsui, D. C. & Gossard, A. C. The fractional quantum Hall effect. Rev. Mod. Phys. 71, S298 (1999)."). A topological phase is unique in that it does not break any of the underlying symmetries of the system and cannot be described by a local order parameter[7](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR7 "Wen, X.-G. Choreographed entanglement dances: topological states of quantum matter. Science 363, eaal3099 (2019)."). In other words, the inherent properties of the system cannot be changed by adiabatic shifts in materials parameters unless the system passes a quantum critical point associated with a phase transition.

Materials exhibiting topological phases arising from bandstructure effects are generally referred to as topological insulators (TIs). Generally speaking, TIs are a class of materials with an insulating time-reversal invariant (TRI) bandstructure for which strong spin–orbit interactions lead to an inversion of the band gap at an odd number of time-reversed points in the Brillouin zone[8](#ref-CR8 "Kane, C. L. & Mele, E. J. Quantum spin Hall effect in graphene. Phys. Rev. Lett. 95, 1757 (2005)."),[9](#ref-CR9 "Hughes, T. L. & Bernevig, B. A. Topological Insulators and Topological Superconductors (Princeton University Press, 2013)."),[10](#ref-CR10 "Hasan, M. Z. & Kane, C. L. Colloquium: topological insulators. Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 3045 (2010)."),[11](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR11 "Qi, X. L. & Zhang, S. C. Topological insulators and superconductors. Rev. Mod. Phys. 83, 1057 (2011)."). There currently exist a series of known topological materials that meet these criteria, such as HgTe/CdTe quantum wells[12](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR12 "Bernevig, B. A., Hughes, T. & Zhang, S.-C. Quantum spin Hall effect and topological phase transition in hgte quantum wells. Science 314, 1757 (2006)."),[13](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR13 "König, M. et al. Quantum spin Hall insulator state in HgTe quantum wells. Science 318, 766 (2007).") and transition metal dichalcogenides Bi2Te3 and Bi2Se3[14](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR14 "Hseih, D. et al. First observation of spin-momentum helical locking in Bi2Se3 and Bi2Te3, demonstration of topological order at 300 k and a realization of topological transport regime. Nature 460, 1101 (2009)."). Due to this TRI band inversion, TIs possess very unique properties, such as spin–momentum locking of the surface states[14](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR14 "Hseih, D. et al. First observation of spin-momentum helical locking in Bi2Se3 and Bi2Te3, demonstration of topological order at 300 k and a realization of topological transport regime. Nature 460, 1101 (2009)."), magneto-electric effect[15](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR15 "Qi, X. L., Li, R., Zang, J. & Zhang, S. C. Inducing a magnetic monopole with topological surface states. Science 323, 1184 (2009)."), and lack of backscattering[16](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR16 "Alpichshev, Z. et al. STM imaging of electronic waves on the surface of Bi2Se3: topologically protected surface states and hexagonal warping effects. Phys. Rev. Lett. 104, 016401 (2010)."). The TI of most technological relevance is Bi2Se3, which has been experimentally shown to be a TI at room temperature with a topologically non-trivial energy gap of 0.35 eV, as observed using angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy[11](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR11 "Qi, X. L. & Zhang, S. C. Topological insulators and superconductors. Rev. Mod. Phys. 83, 1057 (2011)."),[14](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR14 "Hseih, D. et al. First observation of spin-momentum helical locking in Bi2Se3 and Bi2Te3, demonstration of topological order at 300 k and a realization of topological transport regime. Nature 460, 1101 (2009)."). The unique properties of these topological states may also have a significant impact on post-CMOS devices and information processing technologies.

While promising, it remains to be demonstrated that the unique properties of TI may be exploited to enable new device technologies. In this review, we examine the current state of the field in assessing the relevance of TIs in the overall landscape of enabling new electronic and magnetic device technologies within the general overall landscape of both CMOS and beyond-CMOS information processing. More specifically, we will examine the current work in charge transport devices and magnetic devices where each technological implementation is enabled by the unique properties of TIs. Additionally, where possible and appropriate, performance comparisons with other relevant current and next-generation technologies are made so as to quantify the progress that TIs have made and assess their future prospects. We note that, in choosing to focus on the charge and magnetic technologies, we omit mention of the fundamental and important work on TIs for thermoelectric applications. Unquestionably, work on thermoelectric devices falls within the dominion of a review whose stated purpose is to survey the advances in the application of well-characterized TIs for future device technologies, and there exist several excellent reviews of topological thermoelectrics that we recommend to the interested reader without reproducing their work[17](#ref-CR17 "Baldomir, D. & Faílde, D. On behind the physics of the thermoelectricity of topological insulators. Sci. Rep. 9, 1–8 (2019)."),[18](#ref-CR18 "He, J. & Tritt, T. M. Advances in thermoelectric materials research: looking back and moving forward. Science 357, eaak9997 (2017)."),[19](#ref-CR19 "Han, J. et al. Room-temperature spin-orbit torque switching induced by a topological insulator. Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 077702 (2017)."),[20](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR20 "Xu, Y. Thermoelectric effects and topological insulators. Chin. Phys. B 25, 117309 (2016).").

## Topological electronic devices

We begin the survey with a review of the most salient work performed toward using topological materials as the foundation for new types of nanotechnology by examining charge transport in TI as it pertains to relevant CMOS applications. More precisely, we will examine charge transport in active device structures with the purpose of developing (1) new transistor technologies and (2) new local electrical interconnects, again as examined within the limited scope of currently available topological materials rather than those materials that are not yet sufficiently characterized, in which case questions about device applicability are either premature, or the materials that are not broadly available, or both.

### Topological field-effect transistors (FETs)

Given a new material, such as a TI, an obvious application is that of the essential building block underlying all CMOS architectures, the transistor. At first glance, the properties of TIs, and more specifically, Bi2Se3, may lend themselves quite naturally to function effectively as a transistor material due to the inherent lack of backscattering in the topologically protected surface states. Therefore, charge transport within the protected surface state has the benefit of being immune to the charged impurity scattering that may deeply affect the properties of nanoscale MOSFETs. Nonetheless, the presence of a metallic surface state raises the immediate question as to how an applied electric field will be able to tune the carrier densities of the TI. Within this context, the path to the realization of a TI transistor begins with the earliest attempts to examine FET structures using Bi2Se3 as the channel material. The initial studies utilized cleaved bulk Bi2Se3 films of thickness ≈100 nm in dual gate configuration where there is a back gate and a top gate that may be used to independently tune the carrier densities[21](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR21 "Liu, H. & Ye, P. D. Atomic-layer-deposited al2o3 on bi2te3 for topological insulator field-effect transistors. Appl. Phys. Lett. 99, 052108 (2011)."),[22](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR22 "Steinberg, H., Gardner, D. R., Lee, Y. S. & Jarillo-Herrero, P. Surface state transport and ambipolar electric field effect in Bi2Se3 nanodevices. Nano Lett. 10, 5032 (2010)."). The fabricated devices show evidence of three parallel conductive channels resulting from the two surface states and the populated bulk states. However, despite the presence of significant bulk carriers, electric field gating is capable of altering the dominant carrier type from electrons to holes, which is a fundamental prerequisite for eventual CMOS implementation[22](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR22 "Steinberg, H., Gardner, D. R., Lee, Y. S. & Jarillo-Herrero, P. Surface state transport and ambipolar electric field effect in Bi2Se3 nanodevices. Nano Lett. 10, 5032 (2010).").

An obvious problem arises due to the presence of 1018–1019 cm−3 bulk states resulting from the effects of unintentional selenium vacancy doping[22](#ref-CR22 "Steinberg, H., Gardner, D. R., Lee, Y. S. & Jarillo-Herrero, P. Surface state transport and ambipolar electric field effect in Bi2Se3 nanodevices. Nano Lett. 10, 5032 (2010)."),[23](#ref-CR23 "Hor, Y. et al. p-type Bi2Se3 for topological insulator and low-temperature thermoelectric applications. Phys. Rev. B 79, 195208 (2009)."),[24](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR24 "Cho, S., Butch, N. P., Paglione, J. & Fuhrer, M. S. Insulating behavior in ultrathin bismuth selenide field effect transistors. Nano Lett. 11, 1925 (2011)."). The presence of such states is deleterious to FET operation as it provides a less resistive path for current flow as compared to the higher resistance two-dimensional (2D) topological surface states. Therefore, in such a situation, one would expect that the majority of the current would be shunted through the ample bulk states inhibiting the observation of benefits resulting from conduction via the topological surface states. A potential route to minimize the contribution of the trivial bulk electrons is to utilize the confinement of thin TI samples for FET devices. FETs consisting of ultra-thin (thickness 3–14 nm) exfoliated Bi2Se3 on 300 nm SiO2/Si substrates that also function as the gate electrode[24](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR24 "Cho, S., Butch, N. P., Paglione, J. & Fuhrer, M. S. Insulating behavior in ultrathin bismuth selenide field effect transistors. Nano Lett. 11, 1925 (2011)."). In Fig. [1a](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#Fig1), we display measured plots of the conductance of a 3.5-nm-thick Bi2Se3 FET at a variety of different temperatures. While the FET did not produce evidence of drain current saturation or ambipolar conduction characteristic of traditional semiconductor FETs, the transport measurements confirm that, with sufficient gate voltage, the thin-film Bi2Se3 may be depleted of electrons, both on the surface and in the bulk, to form an insulator.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42005-021-00569-5/MediaObjects/42005_2021_569_Fig1_HTML.png)

**a** Experimental measurements of the conductance versus the applied gate voltage showing metallic and insulating behavior of ultra-thin 3D topological insulator Bi2Se3 FETs at a variety of different temperatures. Reprinted (adapted) with permission from ref. [24](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR24 "Cho, S., Butch, N. P., Paglione, J. & Fuhrer, M. S. Insulating behavior in ultrathin bismuth selenide field effect transistors. Nano Lett. 11, 1925 (2011)."), copyright 2011. **b** Schematic illustration of the design of a gate-all-around (GAA) design of a nanowire FET fabricated in Bi2Se3[29](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR29 "Zhu, H. et al. Topological insulator Bi2Se3 nanowire high performance field-effect transistor. Sci. Rep. 3, 1757 (2013)."). **c** The thermal evolution of the experimentally extracted effective mobility within the channel of the Bi2Se3 nanowire FET as a function of the applied gate voltage[29](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR29 "Zhu, H. et al. Topological insulator Bi2Se3 nanowire high performance field-effect transistor. Sci. Rep. 3, 1757 (2013)."). We immediately notice that the mobility is far less than typical semiconductors currently used in CMOS devices. **d** The drain terminal current, *I*DS, plotted against the applied drain voltage, *V*DS, at *T*sys = 77 K demonstrating that typical transistor transfer curves may be obtained in topological FETs[29](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR29 "Zhu, H. et al. Topological insulator Bi2Se3 nanowire high performance field-effect transistor. Sci. Rep. 3, 1757 (2013)."). **e** The sub-threshold slope as extracted from nanowire topological FETs (solid line) showing that the incarnation of the topological FET requires more energy to switch from the “off” state to the “on” state than an ideal FET. Reprinted by permission from ref. [29](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR29 "Zhu, H. et al. Topological insulator Bi2Se3 nanowire high performance field-effect transistor. Sci. Rep. 3, 1757 (2013)."), copyright 2013.

It is clear that reducing the thickness of the TI films to reduce the bulk conductivity allows for subsequent electrostatic gating that is capable of removing much of the bulk doping without inducing breakdown. Therefore, the simple production of cleaved thin-film TI samples is sufficient to produce the “off” state that results from the complete depletion of bulk doping. Unfortunately, reduced sample size alone is insufficient to be able to change the dominant doping from *n*-type to *p*-type in thin-film TI samples[24](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR24 "Cho, S., Butch, N. P., Paglione, J. & Fuhrer, M. S. Insulating behavior in ultrathin bismuth selenide field effect transistors. Nano Lett. 11, 1925 (2011)."). In an effort to shift the chemical potential further toward the edge of the conduction band edge, improvements to TI-FET devices examined the incorporation Ca dopants that substitute for the Bi atoms into Bi2Se3 crystals[25](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR25 "Checkelsky, J. G., Hor, Y. S., Cava, R. J. & Ong, N. P. Bulk band gap and surface state conduction observed in voltage-tuned crystals of the topological insulator Bi2Se3. Phys. Rev. Lett. 106, 196801 (2011)."). For ultra-thin FET geometries, the addition of Ca atoms to the material reduces the bulk carrier concentration so that electrostatic gating switches n-type behavior to p-type. The Ca-doped samples still exhibit parallel conduction through bulk and surface states, nonetheless the bulk conductivity is smaller than the surface contribution up to temperatures of *T*sys = 130 K[25](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR25 "Checkelsky, J. G., Hor, Y. S., Cava, R. J. & Ong, N. P. Bulk band gap and surface state conduction observed in voltage-tuned crystals of the topological insulator Bi2Se3. Phys. Rev. Lett. 106, 196801 (2011)."). Additionally, one may be concerned about the formation of a bulk impurity band within the TI bulk band gap resulting from the addition of high dopant concentrations. Yet, the possibility of an induced bulk impurity band is excluded as a potential factor that may occlude the observation of surface state conduction due to the fact that bulk impurity bands are normally populated by carriers that are of lower carrier mobility than the measured bulk mobility. In a similar manner, thin-film FET behavior is observed in films grown by molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) where surface state conductivity dominates for film thicknesses from 2 to 256 nm for *T*sys < 30 K indicating that not only sample preparation but also sample growth conditions play a role in the resultant transfer characteristics[26](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR26 "Bansal, N., Kim, Y. S., Brahlek, M., Edrey, E. & Oh, S. Thickness-independent transport channels in topological insulator Bi2Se3 thin films. Phys. Rev. Lett. 106, 196801 (2011)."). While the thin-film geometry offers a simple path to the reduction of the deleterious bulk states, within the TRI topological materials we consider in this review, there is a clear limit to the reduction in thickness possible. Experiments have clearly demonstrated that when the thickness of three-dimensional (3D) topological materials is reduced below 5 quintuple layers (QLs), where one QL is 0.955 nm in thickness. Below these thicknesses, there is an appreciable intersurface tunneling between the normally isolated surfaces that leads to the formation of a gap in the energy spectrum[27](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR27 "Taskin, A. A., Sasaki, S., Segawa, K. & Ando, Y. Manifestation of topological protection in transport properties of epitaxial Bi2Se3 thin films. Phys. Rev. Lett. 109, 066803 (2012)."). Yet, there is theoretical work that points to the fact that the gapped thin-film TIs have distinct properties from the thicker materials such as a vanishing in-plane spin component and Berry curvature close to the gap edge in momentum space. Exploiting these new properties, should they be experimentally accessible, may lead to new applicability of thin-film TIs once they are more clearly understood[28](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR28 "Thalmeier, P. & Akbari, A. Gapped Dirac cones and spin texture in thin film topological insulator. Phys. Rev. Res. 2, 033002 (2020).").

The culmination of early examinations of topological FETs focuses on utilizing design that is closely connected to current CMOS transistors by utilizing Bi2Se3 nanowires in the gate-all-around (GAA) configuration, shown schematically in Fig. [1b](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#Fig1). The International Roadmap for Devices and Systems (IRDS) predicts that the GAA transistor design will be in use in nanoscale FETs by 2024[1](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR1 "Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), The International Roadmap for Devices and Systems (IRDS). 2018 (2018)."). In the GAA configuration, single-crystal Bi2Se3 nanowires of various sizes ranging from 50 to 150 nm wide with a corresponding length of 10 μm are surrounded by a HfO2 gate dielectric and subsequently covered by a Pd gate metal[29](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR29 "Zhu, H. et al. Topological insulator Bi2Se3 nanowire high performance field-effect transistor. Sci. Rep. 3, 1757 (2013)."). The source and drain contacts are Ti/Pt and the entire structure is built upon a SiO2/Si layer beneath the FET. Subsequent electrical characterization of the FET is performed at *T*sys = 77 K. The TI nanowire FET shows similar unipolar conduction dominated by electrons, as one may expect from Se vacancy doping[23](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR23 "Hor, Y. et al. p-type Bi2Se3 for topological insulator and low-temperature thermoelectric applications. Phys. Rev. B 79, 195208 (2009)."),[24](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR24 "Cho, S., Butch, N. P., Paglione, J. & Fuhrer, M. S. Insulating behavior in ultrathin bismuth selenide field effect transistors. Nano Lett. 11, 1925 (2011)."), and an excellent *I*on/*I*off ratio that exceeds 108, albeit at a temperature that is far lower than room temperature. In Fig. [1c](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#Fig1), we show the *I*DS–*V*DS curves that show behavior similar to the cutoff, linear, and saturation regimes of a semiconductor MOSFET; however, the saturation regime in the *I*DS–*V*DS curves occurs not due to pinch-off of the nanowire at the drain end of the FET, as is the case in a conventional MOSFET, but due to velocity saturation at the source end of the FET. Nonetheless, recent improvements in crystal growth techniques have seen the fabrication of p–n junctions in Bi2Se3 that allow for ambipolar conduction with physics identical in form to conventional semiconductor FET electrostatics, but it is unclear as to how such as process takes advantage of any unique topological characteristics[30](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR30 "Satake, Y., Shiogai, J., Fujiwara, K. & Tsukazaki, A. Effect of the depletion region in topological insulator heterostructures for ambipolar field-effect transistors. Phys. Rev. B 98, 125415 (2018).").

The *I*DS–*V*DS curves produced by the GAA topological FET are encouraging in that the results demonstrate that TIs may potentially be used as effective channel replacement materials in future CMOS applications. Yet, there are two important measures that have not been addressed in the examination of the topological FET: carrier mobility within the transistor and the sub-threshold slope. In Fig. [1d](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#Fig1), the effective carrier mobility, extracted from the linear portion of the *I*DS–*V*DS curve in Fig. [1c](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#Fig1), is plotted for a variety of different gate voltages at temperatures ranging from *T*sys = 77 K to *T*sys = 240 K. At *T*sys = 77 K, the electron mobility in the transistor is *μ*eff = 1300 cm2/V⋅s but decreases appreciably to *μ*eff = 100 cm2/V⋅s as the temperature approaches room temperature, which is both indicative of both the dominance of phonon scattering in setting the value of the mobility and the significantly reduced value as compared to silicon nanowires of the same dimensionality at room temperature[1](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR1 "Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), The International Roadmap for Devices and Systems (IRDS). 2018 (2018)."). Additionally, one of the most important figures of merit for a transistor implementation is the sub-threshold swing, *S*, or the change in gate voltage required to change the current by one order of magnitude. In Fig. [1f](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#Fig1), the sub-threshold swing of the topological transistor is plotted as a function of temperature. One quickly observes that the value of *S* for the topological transistor is significantly greater than that of the ideal transistor at the same temperature. Taken in tandem, Fig. [1d, e](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#Fig1) demonstrate that, for the topological FET to compete with current CMOS technology or band–band tunneling transistors[31](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR31 "Ionescu, A. M. & Riel, H. Tunnel field-effect transistors as energy-efficient electronic switches. Nature 479, 329 (2011)."),[32](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR32 "Sarkar, D. et al. A subthermionic tunnel field-effect transistor with an atomically thin channel. Nature 526, 91 (2015)."), there are many materials and fabrication issues that are yet to be solved, if possible.

Corresponding theoretical work on the overall relevance of TI for FET applications has, to this point, rendered inconclusive results as to the eventual benefit derived from utilizing TI in transistor applications. The results suggest that ultra-thin TI films, in which the top and bottom surface states interact to open a gap, may either provide an excellent material for FET implementations[33](/articles/s42005-021-00569-5#ref-CR33 "Akhavan, N. D., Jolley, G., Umana-Membreno

enriched_points.expanded_content: Topological insulators uniquely conduct electricity on their surfaces while remaining insulating in their bulk, allowing for lossless transport of electrons, crucial for future electronics.
Topological insulators exhibit resilience against surface defects and impurities, maintaining conductive states due to their topological nature, which preserves electron pathways.

Key performance metrics for topological insulators include high spin Hall conductivities and low thermal conductivities, making them promising materials for spintronic and thermoelectric applications.
